content.text: Azure Hybrid Benefit

Azure Hybrid Benefit (AHB) is a licensing program that lets you apply existing on-premises Windows Server and SQL Server licenses — covered by Software Assurance (SA) or active subscriptions — to Azure resources. Instead of paying for the operating system or database engine as part of the Azure resource price, you bring your own license and pay only for the underlying compute.
The savings can be substantial: up to 40% on Windows VMs, up to 55% on Azure SQL Managed Instance, and up to 85% when AHB is stacked with a 3-year Reserved Instance.

attrs.alt: Azure Hybrid Benefit coverage and savings diagram

content.text: Eligible Licenses and Coverage Rules
AHB is not automatic — you must have qualifying licenses and must declare the benefit explicitly on each resource.
License Type
Azure Coverage
Requirements
Windows Server Standard (2-core pack)
2 VMs, up to 16 cores each
Software Assurance required
Windows Server Datacenter (2-core pack)
Unlimited VMs of any size
Software Assurance required
SQL Server Enterprise (per core)
4 vCores in Azure SQL / MI / VM
SA or active subscription
SQL Server Standard (per core)
1 vCore in Azure SQL / MI / VM
SA or active subscription
RHEL subscription
RHEL VMs (bring RHEL subscription)
Active Red Hat subscription
SLES subscription
SLES VMs
Active SUSE subscription

Windows Server Standard licenses have a dual-use right: you can run the on-premises workload AND the Azure VM simultaneously for up to 180 days during migration, then must remove the on-premises instance.
Enabling AHB on Windows VMs

# Enable AHB when creating a new Windows VM
az vm create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name win-server-01 \
  --image Win2022Datacenter \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --license-type Windows_Server \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --admin-password "<password>"

# Enable AHB on an existing Windows VM
az vm update \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name win-server-01 \
  --license-type Windows_Server

# Verify the license type
az vm show \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name win-server-01 \
  --query "licenseType" \
  --output tsv

# Enable AHB on all Windows VMs in a resource group (batch)
az vm list \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --query "[?storageProfile.osDisk.osType=='Windows'].name" \
  --output tsv | while read vm; do
    echo "Applying AHB to $vm"
    az vm update \
      --resource-group <rg-name> \
      --name "$vm" \
      --license-type Windows_Server
done
Enabling AHB on SQL Resources
SQL Server AHB applies to three Azure services: Azure SQL Database, Azure SQL Managed Instance, and SQL Server on Azure VMs. Each has a different CLI parameter.

# Azure SQL Database: enable AHB (license-type = BasePrice)
az sql db update \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --server <sql-server-name> \
  --name <db-name> \
  --license-type BasePrice

# Azure SQL Managed Instance: enable AHB
az sql mi update \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --name <mi-name> \
  --license-type BasePrice

# SQL Server on Azure VM: register with AHB
az sql vm update \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --name <vm-name> \
  --license-type AHUB

# Check current license type
az sql mi show \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --name <mi-name> \
  --query "licenseType" \
  --output tsv

BasePrice is the AHB flag for Azure SQL resources; it means you bring your SQL Server license and pay only for compute. LicenseIncluded is the default (Microsoft provides the license, at higher cost).
Enabling AHB on VM Scale Sets

# Create a VMSS with AHB enabled
az vmss create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name win-vmss \
  --image Win2022Datacenter \
  --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --instance-count 3 \
  --license-type Windows_Server \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --admin-password "<password>"

# Update an existing VMSS to enable AHB
az vmss update \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name win-vmss \
  --license-type Windows_Server

# Apply the change to running instances
az vmss update-instances \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name win-vmss \
  --instance-ids "*"
Auditing AHB Coverage with Azure Policy
At scale, manually tracking which resources have AHB enabled is error-prone. Azure Policy can audit or enforce AHB on all eligible VMs and SQL resources in a subscription or management group.

# List built-in AHB-related policy definitions
az policy definition list \
  --query "[?contains(displayName, 'Hybrid') || contains(displayName, 'hybrid')].{Name:name, Display:displayName}" \
  --output table

# Assign the 'Windows VMs should use Azure Hybrid Benefit' audit policy
az policy assignment create \
  --name "audit-ahb-windows-vms" \
  --display-name "Audit: Windows VMs should use Azure Hybrid Benefit" \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/fc9b3da7-8347-4380-8e70-0a0361d8dedd" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --enforcement-mode Default

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

# Audit all Windows VMs: which have AHB and which don't
ahb_enabled = []
ahb_missing = []

for vm in client.virtual_machines.list_all():
    os_type = vm.storage_profile.os_disk.os_type if vm.storage_profile else None
    if str(os_type) == "OperatingSystemTypes.Windows":
        if vm.license_type == "Windows_Server":
            ahb_enabled.append(vm.name)
        else:
            ahb_missing.append({"name": vm.name, "location": vm.location, "size": vm.hardware_profile.vm_size if vm.hardware_profile else "unknown"})

print(f"AHB enabled: {len(ahb_enabled)} VMs")
print(f"AHB missing: {len(ahb_missing)} VMs (potential savings lost)")
for vm in ahb_missing[:10]:
    print(f"  {vm['name']} ({vm['location']}, {vm['size']})")
Combining AHB with Reserved Instances

AHB and Reserved Instances (RIs) stack: you apply AHB to remove the OS license cost, then apply an RI to discount the compute cost. This combination delivers the maximum possible savings on a given resource.
Configuration
Approximate Cost
vs PAYG
PAYG (license included)
Baseline
AHB only (bring Windows license)
RI only (1-year, license included)
AHB + 1-year RI
AHB + 3-year RI

Numbers above are approximate for a Standard_D4s_v5 in East US. Actual savings vary by region and SKU. Apply AHB first (it reduces the per-hour rate), then purchase an RI against the AHB-discounted base.

# Purchase a 1-year RI for a Windows VM that already has AHB
# The RI applies to the compute component; AHB covers the OS
az reservations reservation-order purchase \
  --apply-scope-type Shared \
  --billing-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --display-name "win-d4sv5-1yr-ahb" \
  --quantity 3 \
  --reserved-resource-type VirtualMachines \
  --sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --term P1Y \
  --billing-plan Upfront \
  --location eastus
Tracking AHB Savings in Cost Management

Cost Management does not show a separate AHB line item. Savings are implicit — the billed rate for an AHB resource is lower than the standard rate. To measure savings, compare the AHB resource's actual cost against the standard (license-included) price for the same SKU.
AHB with Bicep and ARM Templates

When deploying infrastructure as code, set the licenseType property in your Bicep or ARM template so AHB is consistently applied to every VM provisioned, rather than relying on manual post-deployment steps.
Linux AHB: RHEL and SLES

AHB also applies to Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) and SUSE Linux Enterprise Server (SLES) VMs. If you have existing Red Hat or SUSE subscriptions (purchased directly, not through Azure Marketplace), you can bring them to Azure VMs and avoid the per-hour subscription charge.

# Create a RHEL VM with AHB (bring your Red Hat subscription)
az vm create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name rhel-worker-01 \
  --image RHEL \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --license-type RHEL_BYOS \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Create a SLES VM with AHB
az vm create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name sles-worker-01 \
  --image SLES \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --license-type SLES_BYOS \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Update an existing RHEL VM to use AHB
az vm update \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name rhel-worker-01 \
  --license-type RHEL_BYOS

RHEL_BYOS and SLES_BYOS require that the VM image was deployed from a BYOS (Bring Your Own Subscription) image in the Marketplace, not a standard pay-as-you-go image. You must attach your Red Hat or SUSE subscription to the VM via Red Hat Cloud Access or SUSE Public Cloud after deployment.
AHB for Azure Stack HCI

Azure Stack HCI clusters running on-premises can use AHB to waive the Windows Server Datacenter license cost for VMs running on the cluster. This applies when you have active Software Assurance coverage for Windows Server Datacenter on the physical host cores.

# Enable AHB for a VM on Azure Stack HCI (via Azure Arc)
az connectedmachine update \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name <arc-machine-name> \
  --license-type Windows_Server

# List all Arc-connected machines and their license types
az connectedmachine list \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --query "[].{Name:name, OS:osType, LicenseType:licenseType}" \
  --output table
License Compliance and the Azure Hybrid Benefit Dashboard

Microsoft expects you to track and comply with license assignments. Applying AHB to more resources than you have licenses for is a compliance violation. The Azure Hybrid Benefit dashboard in the Azure Portal (under Cost Management + Billing) shows how many cores are covered by AHB and helps you verify you are not over-claiming.
Windows Server Standard (2-core pack): each pack covers 16 cores across 2 VMs. A Standard_D8s_v5 (8 cores) requires one 2-core pack; the remaining 8 cores need a second pack.
Windows Server Datacenter: covers an unlimited number of VMs on the physical host, but each 2-core pack must correspond to 2 licensed cores on the host.
SQL Server Enterprise: 1 core license = 4 vCores in Azure. A 16-vCore SQL MI requires 4 Enterprise core licenses.
SQL Server Standard: 1 core license = 1 vCore. A 16-vCore SQL MI requires 16 Standard core licenses (or 4 Enterprise).

# Query all AHB-enabled VMs to verify core counts against your license inventory
az vm list \
  --query "[?licenseType == 'Windows_Server'].{Name:name, Location:location, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize, License:licenseType}" \
  --output table

# For SQL resources
az sql mi list \
  --query "[?licenseType == 'BasePrice'].{Name:name, Cores:vCores, License:licenseType}" \
  --output table

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
compute_client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

# Tally total vCores covered by AHB Windows VMs
ahb_vms = []
for vm in compute_client.virtual_machines.list_all():
    if vm.license_type == "Windows_Server":
        # Get vCPU count from VM size
        size = vm.hardware_profile.vm_size if vm.hardware_profile else "unknown"
        ahb_vms.append({"name": vm.name, "size": size, "location": vm.location})

print(f"AHB-enabled Windows VMs: {len(ahb_vms)}")
for v in ahb_vms:
    print(f"  {v['name']} | {v['size']} | {v['location']}")
print()
print("Verify your SA/license inventory covers the total vCore count above.")
print("Windows Server Standard: 1 license per 2 VMs (up to 16 cores each).")
print("Windows Server Datacenter: unlimited VMs per physical host's licensed cores.")
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Applying AHB without SA coverage
License compliance violation; potential audit penalty
Verify SA expiry dates before enabling AHB
Forgetting to enable AHB on new deployments
Paying license-included rate unnecessarily
Use Azure Policy (audit/deny) to enforce AHB on Windows VMs
Enabling AHB on SQL without enough core licenses
Over-claiming licenses; compliance risk
Count vCores in Azure SQL MI/DB and verify against SQL Server license inventory
Not applying AHB to VMSS
Scale-out instances pay full rate
Set license-type in VMSS model and run update-instances
Confusing RHEL_BYOS and RHEL_SAPHA
Wrong license type, no actual discount
RHEL_BYOS = bring your own subscription; RHEL_SAPHA = for RHEL with Software Assurance (different)
Automating AHB Remediation with Azure Policy

Rather than auditing and manually fixing VMs, you can deploy a DeployIfNotExists policy to automatically apply AHB whenever a new Windows VM is created without it. This enforces compliance proactively rather than reactively.

# Deploy the policy definition and assignment via CLI
az policy definition create \
  --name "enforce-ahb-windows-vms" \
  --display-name "Enforce Azure Hybrid Benefit on Windows VMs" \
  --mode Indexed \
  --rules policy-rule.json

az policy assignment create \
  --name "enforce-ahb" \
  --display-name "Enforce AHB on Windows VMs" \
  --policy "enforce-ahb-windows-vms" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --assign-identity \
  --identity-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --role "Virtual Machine Contributor" \
  --location eastus

The policy's managed identity needs Virtual Machine Contributor role on the scope so it can update the licenseType property. After assignment, run a remediation task to fix existing non-compliant VMs: az policy remediation create --policy-assignment enforce-ahb --name remediate-existing --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance
Summary

Azure Hybrid Benefit lets you reuse existing Windows Server and SQL Server licenses covered by Software Assurance or active subscriptions, reducing Azure VM costs by up to 40% and SQL costs by up to 55%. Enable it via the --license-type flag on VMs, VMSS, and SQL resources. Use Azure Policy to audit and enforce AHB coverage at scale, and stack AHB with Reserved Instances to achieve up to 85% total savings on long-running workloads.
